dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for cardiac arrest.
Ma'am, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. We're here for your brother. Can we get some details about what happened today before we start assessments?
He was found unresponsive in bed. Family was doing CPR before we arrived. They had given him two Narcan shots earlier.
Ma'am, would you check his response currently? No eye opening, no movement, and he's not making any verbal sounds.
GCS of 3. Patient is not responding to any stimuli. Eyes, verbal, and motor are zero. He is pulseless and apneic.
We need to secure his airway. We are going to use a King Laryngeal Airway or an i-gel now. Would you please step back for this procedure?
Airway has been secured with the King device. We are hearing good bilateral breath sounds. A natural airway may not be viable due to his condition.
He is receiving ventilation through the King airway. Capnography is consistently in the mid-30s, and CO2 is responding well with BVM. We are ventilating at 12 breaths per minute.
We are going to insert an intraosseous line. Would one of you help us with that, ma'am?
IO site is placed at the left humeral bone. The intraosseous access is up and ready for medication.
Initially no pulse. During ROSC, a strong pulse of 121 was recorded. I've updated the vitals now.
No respiration at first. With the airway secured, we're recording 12 per minute with good depth.
Blood pressure was unmeasurable initially due to no pulse. After ROSC, it was 139/68 mmHg.
Glucose at 268. His diabetes may be a contributing factor. No further monitoring was done due to instability.
SpO2 is at 0%. He is on BVM with good capnography. We are not relying on the pulse ox at this point.
Initial EKG showed sinus tachycardia but then dropped into asystole. We later saw it transition into PEA at 17:36. This suggests a cardiac issue not responsive to initial treatment.
He was last seen normal about 10 minutes ago. He was resting in bed when it happened suddenly.
He had hypertension and non-insulin diabetes. He took Percocet and Amitriptyline.
Those were the only medications in the room. He had no visible polypharmacy.
We found him unresponsive. He had a history of drug use, so likely overdose. Narcan didn't work on him yet.
No pain reported, as far as we could tell.
There was no indication of pain at time of arrest. The family confirmed no pain before or at presentation.
Skin is very pale and slightly warm. No bruises, cuts, or rashes observed.
Ears, nose, and throat show no rashes or sores. Family also noted none before the incident.
Back and extremities show no signs of trauma or injury. He was at rest in bed.
Current pulse rate is 94. Possible decline in cardiovascular function.
Respiration is at 11 per minute and shallow. We are closely tracking.
Blood pressure is now 55/37. That is quite low and a cause for high concern.
Still reading 0% with BVM. Oxygenation is unchanged.
Autopulse is being used for mechanical CPR to try to maintain circulation.
We performed 4 lead and 12 lead ECG for monitoring for MI.
We're keeping the King airway in place for airway support, as was initially secured with an i-gel.
First epinephrine via IO given and there was no response to it.
A transition to PEA occurred at 17:36. This suggests a cardiac issue not responsive to initial treatment.
Continuing with cardiac arrest protocol. ROSC occurred but was not sustained.
Epinephrine was given again to manage pulseless episodes.
Down to 97 by 18:04. ROSC was non-sustained again.
12 breaths per minute, ongoing with BVM assistance.
Blood pressure is 74/47. Very low despite the interventions.
SpO2 is unchanged at 0%. Continue relying on capnography.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm. But there have been arrests earlier in transport.
Transport to VCU with critical priority. It's the most appropriate facility.
Patient is now on the Reeves stretcher with secure restraints. No issues in transport.
Glucose remains at 268. Watch for complications related to that.
Mechanical ventilation continues with the BVM at 12 per minute.
Mechanical CPR resumed. Capnography remains steady with BVM.
SpO2 is still at 0, as expected in this condition.
EKG is still in sinus. But earlier episodes are an ongoing concern.
Autopulse used again to support circulation. BVM remains active.
12 lead ECG completed for final cardiac imagery on scene.
Two more epinephrine doses given through the IO en route. We expect no improvement.
Respiration is at 12 per minute with BVM. It's stable, but there is concern.
Last pulse was 97. Expect more changes soon.
Still 74/47. It remains low despite interventions and support.
No improvement in SpO2. It's still at 0%.
Glucose is at 268. Watching for complications from that.
ROSC occurred at 18:02 but was weak and hypotensive. He needs ongoing support.
We're continuing per protocol 2-7. Staying sharp for any more changes.
No more meds planned. Focused on monitoring for any further changes.
CPR ongoing as per the protocol. No end in sight yet.
He is securely strapped on the stretcher. Transport is proceeding smoothly.
VCU is the destination. There are no other facilities that fit his condition.
Pulse at 97, showing little to no improvement.
Still 12 per minute with BVM. We keep supporting.
BP is at 74/47. Despite all, still very low.
Last EKG at 18:04 showed sinus with some signs of infarct.
ROSC confirmed and VCU is aware. No more epinephrine planned until further assessment.
All actions follow the cardiac arrest protocol. Will proceed as directed.
